forgery.short: If you painted a copy of the Mona Lisa and sold it to a museum, claiming it was the original, your painting could be called a forgery, and the crime you've committed is also forgery.

formidable.definition: extremely impressive in strength or excellence
formidable.short: People who are formidable inspire fear and respect thanks to their size, or special ability, or unusual  qualities. If you're a wildly popular celebrity, you probably don't go anywhere without a formidable bodyguard.

fraud.definition: intentional deception resulting in injury to another person
fraud.short: A fraud is a something that deceives or tricks another person, usually to get their money. Frauds are dishonest.

frenzy.short: In a frenzy, people go into a state of hysteria. Stay away from hungry sharks or kids at a 16th birthday party when the pizza arrives if you don't want to get caught up in a feeding frenzy.

fret.short: When you fret, you worry so much about something that it eats away at you. Many people fret about taking standardized tests, but really, they're nothing to sweat.

frivolous.definition: not serious in content or attitude or behavior
frivolous.short: Frivolous things are silly or unnecessary. If something is frivolous, then you don't need it.

frown.definition: a facial expression of dislike or displeasure
frown.short: If you frown at someone, you look at them with disapproval, and if you frown on something they do, you don't approve of that either. Because you frown on smoking, you probably frown at smokers too!

gaffe.definition: a socially awkward or tactless act
gaffe.short: A gaffe is a mistake that embarrasses you in front of others. If you run into a friend out with her grey-haired father, and you blurt out, "Oh, hi, you must be Tara's grandfather!" then you've made a gaffe.

gauche.short: Use the word gauche when you want to call something tacky, graceless, tactless, rude, boorish, or awkward and foolish. Have you just pointed out someone's misuse of this word? Oh dear, how gauche!

gaudy.short: Something that's gaudy is showy, bright and definitely tacky. So think twice about that gaudy rainbow-colored suit and shiny gold shoes ensemble.

gavel.definition: a small mallet used by a presiding officer or a judge
gavel.short: You know that wooden hammer a judge slams down on his desk when he's trying to bring order to the court? That's a gavel.

ghastly.short: Something that's ghastly isn't just gross. It's shockingly, horrifyingly unpleasant — so gruesome and grisly that it makes you want to puke.

gloat.short: If you gloat, you express great satisfaction at the misfortune of others. If your team scores a big win, it would be better not to gloat. Be happy for your win, but don't laugh at the other team's loss.

gorge.definition: a deep ravine, usually with a river running through it
gorge.short: A gorge is a very deep crevice between two mountains or hills. Gorges are formed by rivers running through and eroding rock over a very long period of time.

gossamer.definition: a gauze fabric with an extremely fine texture
gossamer.short: Gossamer is something super fine and delicate — like a spider web or the material of a wedding veil.

gravel.short: Gravel is very small, irregular pieces of rock and stone. Your gravel driveway might crunch under your boots as you walk to the mailbox.

grill.definition: a framework of metal bars used as a partition or a grate
grill.short: Not all grilling involves fire and delicious food.  Sometimes, to grill means to ask a person really intense questions, like when you suspect them of doing something wrong and you want to make them confess the truth.

grumble.definition: make complaining remarks or noises under one's breath
grumble.short: When you grumble, you complain about something clearly but quietly, in a low mutter. Some people grumble a hundred times a day, about everything from the weather to the traffic to their bad luck at bowling.

guarded.short: Someone who's guarded is careful, restrained, and maybe a little bit wary. It's normal for a new rescue dog to be guarded when you first bring her home.

guile.definition: shrewdness as demonstrated by being skilled in deception
guile.short: Use the noun guile for cunning, craftiness, and artful duplicity. Acting like you have a job on Wall Street when you're actually unemployed would take a lot of guile.

hallucinate.definition: have illusions; perceive what is not actually there
hallucinate.short: To hallucinate is to see or hear something that's not really there. If you hallucinate, it's a bit like dreaming while being awake.

hamper.definition: prevent the progress or free movement of
hamper.short: Anything that hampers slows progress or makes it difficult to do something. You might think that the presence of your parents hampers your ability to look cool.

harbor.definition: a sheltered port where ships can take on or discharge cargo
harbor.short: A harbor is a safe place providing refuge and comfort. If you're traveling, the harbor provided by a warm hotel is welcome. For ships, a harbor is a sheltered port area shielded from waves, where it's safe to dock.

herbaceous.definition: characteristic of a nonwoody herb or plant part

heretic.definition: a person whose religious beliefs conflict with church dogma
heretic.short: If your friend became interested in Hinduism, with its many gods and rituals, her Catholic mother might be worried that her daughter was a heretic, or a person whose religious beliefs are in contrast to the fundamental beliefs of her church

heterodox.definition: characterized by departure from accepted standards
heterodox.short: Heterodox is from the Greek root words heteros, meaning "the other," and doxa, meaning "opinion." The adjective heterodox was first applied to people who held a different religious opinion from the standard beliefs and teachings.

hoax.short: If you put on big fake feet, stomp through your muddy backyard and tell everyone you saw Bigfoot fixing a steak on your grill, you are playing a hoax on your friends.

holster.definition: a sheath for carrying a handgun
holster.short: The noun holster looks like holder and that's exactly what it is. Some holsters hold a single item, like a gun, and others are more like tool belts, holding many small items.

homeopathy.definition: a method of treating disease with small amounts of remedies that, in large amounts in healthy people, produce symptoms similar to those being treated

hypocritical.definition: professing feelings or virtues one does not have
hypocritical.short: Hypocritical involves acting in a way that goes against your stated beliefs. If you drive drunk despite the fact that you are the president of Students Against Drunk Driving, you are truly hypocritical.

illicit.definition: contrary to accepted morality or convention
illicit.short: Illicit means not allowed by law or custom. It's illegal to kill endangered elephants, but many poachers still engage in the Illicit ivory trade.

impediment.definition: something immaterial that interferes with action or progress
impediment.short: An impediment is anything that slows or blocks progress. It can refer to a physical thing, like a fallen tree in the road, or something more intangible, like how your short stature is an impediment to becoming a professional basketball play

imperative.short: When something absolutely has to be done and cannot be put off, use the adjective imperative.

impromptu.definition: with little or no preparation or forethought
impromptu.short: Some of the best kinds of parties are impromptu ones, when you decide at the last minute to get together. The adjective impromptu describes things done or said without previous thought or preparation.

improvidence.definition: a lack of prudence and care by someone in the management of resources
improvidence.short: Improvidence is a tendency to act rashly, without carefully thinking things through first. It was your own improvidence that led you to spend all your money on video games instead of saving for your upcoming trip to Disneyland.

improvise.definition: manage in a makeshift way; do with whatever is at hand
improvise.short: To improvise means to make something up on the spot, or figure it out as you go. "Our boss decided to improvise his speech at our company meeting and when he started going off-topic, everyone could tell he hadn't prepared in advance."

impunity.short: If doing something usually results in punishment, but you do it with impunity, you will not be punished for the deed. Students are not allowed to chew gum in school, but teachers do it with impunity. Not fair!

inauspicious.short: Reaching into your hat to pull out a rabbit and instead removing a sock can seem like an inauspicious start to your magic show. Inauspicious describes something that seems unpromising or unlikely to be successful.

incidence.definition: the relative frequency of occurrence of something
incidence.short: Incidence means the frequency with which something bad occurs. You’ll hear of “incidences of cancer” or “incidences of war." You hope the incidence is decreasing, not increasing.

incongruous.definition: lacking in harmony or compatibility or appropriateness
incongruous.short: Something that's incongruous is inconsistent or incompatible with something else. Remember that Sesame Street song "One of these things is not like the other"? They were talking about that one thing being incongruous.

incursion.definition: the act of entering some territory or domain
incursion.short: When an army crosses a border into another country for battle, they are making an incursion into enemy territory. An incursion is an invasion as well as an attack.

indelible.definition: not able to be removed or erased
indelible.short: If something is indelible, you better hope you never regret it, like the indelible tattoo of the name of your favorite band or the indelible first impression it might give people you meet years from now, especially if your taste in music ch

indict.short: If you accuse someone of committing an offense, you indict them. A book that indicts the entire education system might lay out all the reasons that schools are failing kids.

indigenous.short: Use indigenous to describe a plant, animal or person that is native or original to an area. Though Switzerland is known for its chocolates, chocolate, which comes from the cocoa plant, is indigenous to South America.

indignant.definition: angered at something unjust or wrong
indignant.short: When you're indignant, you're angry about an unfair situation. If you discovered that a teacher gave ten extra points on a test to all students who sat in the front row, you'd be indignant.

induce.definition: cause to act in a specified manner
induce.short: To induce is to move or lead someone to action. A promise of a lollipop can induce a toddler to do just about anything, even sit down and be polite to Aunt Edna. Older kids need something more substantial.

indulge.short: Indulge is a verb that means "to give in to something," like when you indulge your craving for chocolate by eating a big piece of it.

ineffable.short: Anything ineffable is unspeakably beautiful, moving, or horrible. It’s beyond expression. If something is so powerful or emotional that you can't even describe it, it’s ineffable.

inept.short: A clumsy, incompetent person — or an ineffective action — is inept. When you're inept, you don't know what you're doing or just can't get it done.

inextricable.definition: incapable of being disentangled or untied
inextricable.short: A chain that is very badly knotted and cannot be unraveled is inextricable. You cannot disentangle it.

infiltrate.definition: pass through an enemy line in a military conflict
infiltrate.short: If you infiltrate a group or organization, you join it in order to gain information, like an employee of a donut shop that infiltrates the competition to try to learn its "secret recipe" for its delicious dough.

inflammable.short: Something that is inflammable can be set on fire easily. You dared not light a match after you accidentally spilled gasoline on yourself because you knew you were inflammable.

infuse.definition: fill, as with a certain quality
infuse.short: To infuse is to steep something in a liquid to extract the flavors from it. You'll impress your friends with your cooking if you infuse rosemary and thyme in broth and then use the liquid to marinate chicken.

ingenious.short: Something ingenious shows creativity and inventiveness. If someone compares you to Einstein, they're implying that you, too, are ingenious.

inquisitive.short: If you are inquisitive that means you love to inquire; you’re always asking questions. Don’t become a private detective if you don’t have an inquisitive personality.

insignia.definition: a badge worn to show official position
insignia.short: An insignia is a badge you wear to show your position or affiliation. If you're an Eagle Scout, you can wear that insignia, or, if you're a Red Sox fan, you might wear the team logo as your insignia.

intact.short: If something is intact it's still in one piece. If you'd like your sand castle to remain intact, I suggest you build it away from the water.

intricate.definition: having many complexly arranged elements; elaborate
intricate.short: Intricate things are complex and have many elements: they're not simple. Think of the intricate wiring of a computer's motherboard, or the intricate plot of a movie that you have a hard time following.

invert.definition: turn inside out or upside down
invert.short: When you invert something, you turn it upside down, whether it’s an idea or a test you place face-down on your desk when you are finished.

invigorate.short: Invigorate means to give strength or energy to something or someone. If you're the new cheerleading coach, you can invigorate the team by bringing them your exciting new cheers, and the team’s growing popularity will invigorate them even mo

invoke.definition: request earnestly; ask for aid or protection
invoke.short: To invoke is to call up something such as a law, a higher power, or even a ghost. In court, you might invoke the Fifth Amendment (the right not to say something that will make you look bad) if you don't want to talk.

irate.short: On the anger scale, first comes annoyed, then vexed, then irate. When cartoon characters are irate, they're so mad that smoke comes out of their ears.

jamb.definition: a vertical side piece of a door or window frame
jamb.short: A jamb is one of the upright boards or posts that support a door or window frame. Your front door shuts securely in between two jambs.

jeer.definition: laugh at with contempt and derision
jeer.short: As a noun, jeer is the act of scoffing, taunting, or mocking. Think of it as an anti-cheer.  If you offer cheers for the visiting team and jeers for the home team, you might not be too popular in the stands.

jeopardy.short: To be in jeopardy is to be in danger. Eating three plates of nachos a day may improve your chances of winning your office's nacho-eating contest; unfortunately, it could also put your health in jeopardy.

jest.short: A jest is a joke. Are you a playful prankster? A jocular jokester? A witty wisecracker? Then you are definitely well versed in the art of the jest.

jocular.definition: characterized by jokes and good humor
jocular.short: Do you like to make a lot of jokes? Are you often silly? Are you usually happy? If so, then you are a jocular person.

jovial.definition: full of or showing high-spirited merriment
jovial.short: Use jovial to describe people who show good humor and are full of joy. Santa Claus, with his constant "ho-ho-hoing" is a jovial figure.

judicious.definition: marked by the exercise of common sense in practical matters
judicious.short: If you're judicious, you've got a good head on your shoulders and make good decisions. Humpty Dumpty's decision to sit on a wall? NOT judicious.

lackluster.short: Lackluster is a compound adjective that means what it sounds like: if something is lackluster it lacks luster; in other words, it is without brilliance, shine, or vitality. Think dull.

lasso.definition: a long noosed rope used to catch animals
lasso.short: A lasso is the loop of rope that cowboys use to catch cattle. To be a successful cowboy or cowgirl, you have to learn to throw a lasso while riding a galloping horse.

lavish.short: Lavish means "generous and extravagant" as an adjective and "to give generously" as a verb. If you don't like it when people lavish you with attention, you might appreciate a lavish spread of excellent food instead.

leash.definition: restraint consisting of a rope used to restrain an animal
leash.short: Dog owners who live in the city need a leash, a rope or cord attached to the dog's collar that helps his owner keep him safe and restrained.

leaven.definition: a substance used to produce fermentation in dough
leaven.short: Leaven, as a noun, is that which causes transformation. It’s the source of yeast that makes your bread rise, it’s the risen bread, and it’s anything that changes the nature of something else.

lethargy.definition: inactivity; showing an unusual lack of energy
lethargy.short: Is your pet slug always this comatose, or is this display of lethargy unusual? The word lethargy describes inactivity or a lack of energy.

leviathan.definition: the largest or most massive thing of its kind
leviathan.short: A leviathan is a giant sea creature. It can be real, like a whale, or mythical.

levy.short: Use the word levy as you would use the word tax: “When the state government imposed a levy on soft drinks, thousands of citizens took to the streets in protest.”

ligneous.short: If something's made of wood, or looks like it is, it's ligneous. A tree is a ligneous plant. A stiff politician is a ligneous person.

limerick.definition: a humorous rhymed verse form of five lines
limerick.short: A limerick is a humorous form of poetry that rhymes and has five lines. Many limericks are dirty.

limp.definition: walk impeded by some physical injury
limp.short: If you walk unevenly, you have a limp. Maybe you pulled your hamstring at the annual Thanksgiving Day Football Showdown, or maybe one leg is three inches shorter than the other. Whatever the reason, if your gait is off kilter, you limp.

linen.definition: a fabric woven with fibers from the flax plant
linen.short: Linen is a type of fabric that's sturdy, cool, and absorbent. If you're going to a summer wedding, you should consider wearing a white linen suit. If you're going on a job interview, it's probably not such a great idea.

literal.definition: limited to the explicit meaning of a word or text
literal.short: To describe something as literal is to say that it is exactly what it seems to be. For example, if you put up a literal barrier to keep the world out, you've actually built a real wall.

lizard.definition: relatively long-bodied reptile with legs and a tapering tail
lizard.short: A lizard is a type of reptile that has scales for skin, a long body, a pointy tail, and usually four legs.  And they breathe fire.  Okay, maybe not that last part, but that would be awesome.

loafer.short: If you're a loafer, you avoid working whenever possible, preferring to lounge around relaxing instead. And, if you're too lazy to tie your own shoes, you may prefer to wear loafers — slip-on shoes without laces.

loll.short: To loll means to hang around lazily without doing much at all. It's a great pleasure to loll about in the park instead of going to work. But your boss might have a problem with it.

loom.definition: a textile machine for weaving yarn into a textile
loom.short: Loom has two distinct meanings. First, a loom is a tool for weaving. Second, to loom is to appear or stand over someone in a threatening way. Don’t loom over the person you’re teaching to weave! You'll just make her nervous.

lope.short: Some words are fun to say: lope is one of them. It's also fun to think about, as it means to move with a casual, striding gait. Imagine a horse cantering along with an easy lope. A pleasant image indeed.

lucrative.short: Use lucrative to refer to a business or investment that makes money. While your parents might want you to pursue a lucrative career, you're committed to your plan of becoming a professional mime.

ludicrous.short: Ludicrous things are funny, absurd, or nonsensical. If someone says something silly or far-fetched, you could say "That's ludicrous!"

lull.short: See lull, think "calm." It could be the noun form (like "the lull before the storm") or the verb: one can lull someone by calming them (as in lulling a baby to sleep with a lullaby).

lustrous.short: In shampoo commercials, the hair you see swinging is lustrous. It is brilliant, in the shiny sense.

mace.definition: a ceremonial staff carried as a symbol of office
mace.short: You might see the leader of a royal procession carrying a mace, or ceremonial staff. The word mace can also refer to two very different weapons: a long stick with spikes on the end or a chemical irritant that you spray in someone's eyes. Ou

malapropism.definition: misuse of a word by confusion with one that sounds similar
malapropism.short: A malapropism occurs when you say one word but you mean another, like instead of saying a certain restaurant is prosperous, you say it is preposterous. As you can tell, malapropisms are often humorous, though sometimes the joke is on the sp

malign.short: If you malign someone, you badmouth them — just like the jilted girlfriend who tells the whole school her ex has bad breath and head lice.

martial.short: An adjective referring to the military, the word martial adds a warlike tone to any noun it describes.

meager.definition: deficient in amount or quality or extent
meager.short: Meager means small and often applies to portions. Your mother may only allow you a meager serving of chocolate cake, and you may need to eat a meager serving of veggies in order to deserve that dessert.

meddle.definition: intrude in other people's affairs or business
meddle.short: To meddle is to interfere. You can meddle in someone else's affairs, and you can meddle with someone else's things. Either way, you’re messing with someone else’s stuff and she probably doesn’t like it.

mediocre.short: Mediocre is an adjective that means "merely adequate" or "of only ordinary quality." A "C" is a mediocre grade for students who are fair to middling.

mend.definition: restore by putting together what is torn or broken
mend.short: When you mend something, you fix it or make it better. If you can mend your broken lamp, then it will shine again. If not you'll have to sit in the dark.

mercenary.definition: a person hired to fight for another country than their own
mercenary.short: You might not want to call a mercenary a "hireling" to his face, but a mercenary is, after all, a soldier who gets paid to fight where needed, sometimes taking a heroic stand and other times just wanting payment for fighting.

metaphysics.definition: the philosophical study of being and knowing
metaphysics.short: Metaphysics is the philosophical study of being and knowing. If you have ever contemplated your own existence in the universe, you were dabbling in metaphysics.

minuscule.short: When something is teeny tiny, it is minuscule. If your mother calls your miniskirt minuscule, it probably means she wants you to change into something a bit less revealing.

misnomer.short: A misnomer is a wrong or unsuitable name. It’s a misnomer to call your grandmother “Grandfather,” the same way it’s a misnomer to call a chair with four legs that doesn’t move unless you drag it across the floor, a rocking chair.

misogynist.definition: a misanthrope who dislikes women in particular
misogynist.short: If you're someone who believes women belong in the kitchen and shouldn't be accorded the same respect as men, you might be a misogynist. A misogynist is a person who hates or doesn't trust women.

mistrust.short: To mistrust is to feel uneasy about someone or something. If you mistrust bus drivers, you may decide to walk instead.

moat.definition: ditch dug as a fortification and usually filled with water
moat.short: Not many houses these days have a moat — it's a trench dug outside a castle and is usually full of water. A moat means "Keep out!"

mollycoddle.short: Mollycoddle means to spoil or overindulge something. You can think of mollycoddle as an extreme form of coddle. If you constantly fuss over your dog and serve her homemade food while she's resting on a soft feather bed, you mollycoddle her.

mosaic.definition: design made of small pieces of colored stone or glass
mosaic.short: If you’ve ever seen a piece of artwork where the picture is comprised of little clay tiles, then you’ve seen a mosaic.

narcotic.definition: a drug that produces numbness or stupor
narcotic.short: Use the noun narcotic to talk about a pain relieving or sleep inducing drug and the adjective narcotic to describe anything that has these same effects.

nausea.short: When you feel like you might throw up, that's nausea. I know you're feeling sea sick, but if our boat sinks, nausea will be the least of your problems.

nefarious.short: Describe a person's actions as nefarious if they are evil or wicked. Batman and Superman are always fighting evildoers and stopping their nefarious plots.

neglect.short: Neglect is worse than ignoring something. It's ignoring it, failing to care for it, and probably harming it in the process.

neuralgia.definition: acute spasmodic pain along the course of one or more nerves

nocturnal.definition: belonging to or active during the night
nocturnal.short: If something is nocturnal, it belongs to or is active at night. That includes vampires, owls that like to hunt by moonlight, and that roommate who stays up playing video games until the wee hours of the morning.

nonchalant.definition: marked by casual unconcern or indifference
nonchalant.short: If your friend is acting cool, unconcerned or in an indifferent manner, call him nonchalant — like when he saunters by a group of whispering, giggling girls and just nods and says, "Hey."

nondescript.short: Nondescript is a word used to describe something that isn't special or unusual in any way. You might have trouble finding a nondescript apartment building because it looks exactly like every other apartment building around it.

note.short: A note is a very short piece of writing. You might write a note to your sister on the back of your grocery list, or jot down a quick note reminding yourself to call your grandmother later.

notion.short: If you have a notion that you can swim across the ocean, you are probably wrong. A notion is an idea, often vague and sometimes fanciful.

oaf.definition: an awkward, clumsy, or stupid person
oaf.short: An oaf is the boring, ill-mannered person you hope doesn't show up at your party and send the other guests running for the door.

obeisance.definition: bending the head or body in reverence or submission
obeisance.short: An obeisance is an act, usually physical, showing dutiful obedience. A supplicant might perform obeisance, touching his face to the ground, before humbly asking for help.

obelisk.definition: a stone pillar tapering towards a pyramidal top
obelisk.short: Next time you visit the nation’s capital, you can wow tourists by telling them the Washington Monument is an obelisk — a tall, narrow stone pillar that tapers to a point at the top and commemorates an important person or event.

obese.short: Obese means excessively fat. In medical terms, it means you have a body mass index (BMI) of 30 or more, at which point your body's excess fat begins to threaten your health.

obituary.short: An obituary is the notice of someone's death. It is placed in the newspaper to alert others to the death. It gives a background about the deceased's life, usually listing survivors and giving funeral information.

objurgate.short: How dare you! To objurgate is to scold or reprimand. Don’t objurgate yourself, or beat yourself up if you didn’t know it — it’s an old word that people don’t use much anymore.

objurgation.short: Objurgation is a harsh reprimand or criticism. A teenager who gets home hours after his midnight curfew can expect objurgation from his angry parents.

oblation.definition: the act of contributing to the funds of a church or charity
oblation.short: An oblation is a donation or offering to a religious institution. When you offer an oblation at church, you are symbolically presenting it to God.

obligatory.short: Obligatory describes something you do because you have to, not because you want to. When you buy a car, you have to fill out the obligatory forms. In many homes, saying please and thank you is obligatory.

obliquity.definition: the quality of being deliberately vague or deceptive
obliquity.short: Obliquity is a characteristic of being deliberately indirect or vague, especially to fool or deceive someone. The obliquity of many politicians makes it hard to get a straight answer from them.

oblivion.definition: the state of being disregarded or forgotten
oblivion.short: Oblivion is the state of being forgotten. Your uncle dreamed of being a rock star, but after recording one hit song, he faded into oblivion.

obnoxious.short: If something is obnoxious, it's annoying and unpleasant. Generally, people like to avoid obnoxious folks.

obscure.short: If something is obscure, it's vague and hard to see. Be careful if you're driving in heavy rain — the painted lines can be obscure.

obsession.definition: an unhealthy and compulsive preoccupation with something
obsession.short: If you have an obsession, you're totally fixated on something and unhealthily devoted to it. Some common obsessions include fantasy football leagues, celebrity gossip, and Elvis memorabilia.

obsidian.definition: glass formed by the cooling of lava without crystallization
obsidian.short: Obsidian is a type of black or dark glass formed from cooling lava. So if you find a shiny, hard, dark piece of glass next time you’re hanging around a volcano, it’s probably a piece of obsidian.

obsolete.short: Use the adjective obsolete for something that is out of date. As the Rolling Stones song "Out of Time" goes, "You're obsolete, my baby, my poor old-fashioned baby."

obstetrician.short: If you were born in the United States, probably the first doctor you ever laid eyes on was an obstetrician, a medical doctor who delivers babies.

obstreperous.short: Obstreperous means boisterous, noisy, aggressive, defiant. You’ve probably seen an obstreperous child in the grocery store, pulling away from her mother, screaming at the top of her lungs.

obtrusive.short: The eight-foot-tall, hot pink statue of Minnie Mouse did not belong among the beige couches of the fancy living room. The statue was obtrusive, meaning it stood out in a bad way.

obvious.definition: easily perceived by the senses or grasped by the mind
obvious.short: Something obvious is plain to see and easily understandable. It was obvious that you didn't enjoy your meal because I found it rolled in a napkin and shoved under the rug.

occult.definition: supernatural forces and events and beings collectively
occult.short: Dark and mysterious, the occult is a kind of supernatural power or magic. If you see your neighbor chanting over a giant vat of bubbling brew in the middle of the night, there's a chance he's dabbling in the occult.

oculist.definition: a person skilled in testing for defects of vision
oculist.short: People who lived in previous centuries who had trouble seeing street signs, reading small print, or suddenly had horrible eye pain, would have visited an oculist. This was a doctor who dealt with eye disorders.

odoriferous.definition: emitting a smell, especially an unpleasant smell
odoriferous.short: Something that's odoriferous carries a smell. When you hear someone use the word odoriferous, just hope they're not referring to your breath or armpits.

odorous.short: Anything that has a strong scent can be described as odorous. Usually if you say something is odorous you mean that it smells unpleasant — like an odorous blue cheese made from unpasteurized milk.

offal.definition: viscera and trimmings of a butchered animal
offal.short: If you are into eating "all" of a butchered animal, then animal offal is your thing. In some cultures, the throat, heart, stomach, and brains of animals are considered offal delicacies. Bon appétit!

offertory.definition: the offerings of the congregation at a religious service

ogle.definition: stare or look at, especially with amorous intentions
ogle.short: When you ogle someone, you look at them with love or desire in mind. And if you're a cartoon character, you might also be drooling.

olfactory.definition: of or relating to the sense of smell
olfactory.short: Something olfactory has to do with the sense of smell. The odor from that factory is a symphony of olfactory delights. Not.

oligarchy.definition: a political system governed by a few people
oligarchy.short: Does it ever feel like just a few people have all the power? If it's a government that's run like this, it's an oligarchy. A country that has this form of government is an oligarchy too.

omnipotent.short: If you want to describe someone who can do absolutely anything, reach for the adjective omnipotent.

omnipresent.short: Omnipresent describes something that's everywhere at once, like a deity. If your parents are constantly monitoring what you do, you may feel like they're omnipresent — and look forward to going to college somewhere far, far away.

omnivorous.definition: feeding on both plants and animals
omnivorous.short: An omnivorous animal eats meat and plants — everything on the menu.

onomatopoeia.definition: using words that imitate the sound they denote
onomatopoeia.short: Boom! Bang! Crash! When a word is formed from the sound that an associated thing makes, call it an example of onomatopoeia.

onslaught.short: Onslaught is a military term that refers to an attack against an enemy. It's safe to say that no one wants to be caught on the receiving end of an onslaught, because there will be lots of danger, destruction and probably death.

onus.short: Take the noun, onus, as a formal word for responsibility or obligation. If your teacher assigns onus as a vocabulary word, it puts the onus on you to find out what it means.

opalescent.definition: having a play of lustrous rainbow colors
opalescent.short: If you're familiar with the way that the gemstone opal shimmers like a pearl, then you'll understand that something opalescent reflects the light in the same way.

opaque.definition: not transmitting or reflecting light or radiant energy
opaque.short: Use the adjective opaque either for something that doesn't allow light to pass through (like a heavy curtain) or for something difficult to understand (like bureaucratic gobbledygook).

opiate.short: An opiate is a drug such as morphine or heroin that is derived from opium. Used figuratively, it means something that calms or soothes.

opportune.definition: suitable or advantageous especially for a particular purpose
opportune.short: Use the adjective opportune to describe especially good timing. A snowstorm is an opportune time to make extra money shoveling your neighbor's driveway.

opportunist.definition: a person who places expediency above principle
opportunist.short: Opportunists are people who see a chance to gain some advantage from a situation, often at the expense of ethics or morals. An opportunist seizes every opportunity to improve things for himself.

opprobrious.short: Opprobrious is a heavy-duty word to describe something taunting or shameful. Opprobrious words criticize in a mean, hurtful way.

optician.definition: a worker who makes glasses for remedying defects of vision
optician.short: The person who makes your eye glasses is an optician. They work in the back room of the glasses shop, or at a factory, fabricating the lenses and attaching them to the frames.

optimum.definition: most desirable possible under a restriction
optimum.short: An optimum is the best condition or highest extent of something. He didn't mean to create a science experiment, but the unwashed coffee cup under his desk became the optimum dark, wet environment for growing lots of mold.

optometrist.definition: a person skilled in testing for defects of vision
optometrist.short: An optometrist specializes in testing the eyes and helping correct vision with glasses or contact lenses. If you can’t read anything below the giant E on the doctor's wall chart, don’t worry: an optometrist can help improve how you see.

opulence.definition: wealth as evidenced by sumptuous living
opulence.short: Something with opulence is drenched in wealth and luxury. You'll need gold brocaded curtains, diamond-encrusted watches, and a world-renowned personal chef if you want to add some opulence to your life.

opus.definition: a musical work that has been created
opus.short: An opus is a created work, usually musical in nature. The composer's greatest opus was inspired by the blooming of the spring flowers.

ordinance.short: An ordinance is a local law. Many cities have recently passed ordinances making it illegal to smoke indoors or mandatory for pet owners to curb their dogs.

orientation.definition: the act of determining one's position
orientation.short: Orientation is all about the direction you're facing. A house can have an east-facing orientation. Freshman orientation starts you off in a good direction. Your political orientation means your political outlook.

orifice.definition: an opening, especially one that opens into a bodily cavity
orifice.short: An orifice is an opening or a hole, most often in the body, such as your mouth or your nostril.

orison.short: An orison is a prayer or plea to a deity. You might make an orison if you wanted your sick mom to get better.

ornithologist.short: An ornithologist is a type of zoologist who focuses on birds. If you want to know anything about our fine feathered friends, consult an ornithologist.

ornithology.definition: the branch of zoology that studies birds
ornithology.short: Ornithology is the scientific study of birds.

orthography.definition: representing the sounds of a language by written symbols
orthography.short: If you speak English — and you probably do if you're reading this — an orthography or writing system of "A, B, C, D..." makes sense. The Serbian language uses a different orthography: "A B C Č Ć D Dž Đ..."

ossified.definition: set in a rigidly conventional pattern of behavior or beliefs
ossified.short: Use the adjective ossified to describe someone who's become stuck in his ways. You might feel that your uncle has become an ossified suburban soccer dad, set in his unbending view of the world and comfortable lifestyle.

ostracism.definition: the act of excluding someone from society by general consent
ostracism.short: If you banish someone from society, you've caused that person's ostracism. Quakers, for example, faced ostracism by Puritans in colonial Massachusetts and many of them fled to the nearby colony of Rhode Island.

ostracize.definition: expel from a community or group
ostracize.short: If you banish someone or ignore him, you ostracize him. When the Iranian president claimed that the Holocaust was a hoax, he was ostracized by the international community.

outgrowth.definition: the gradual beginning or coming forth

outmoded.short: Something that is outmoded is no longer cool or in style, like your dad's outmoded hairstyle that he's had since he was in high school.

outset.definition: the time at which something is supposed to begin

overhaul.definition: make repairs, renovations, revisions or adjustments to
overhaul.short: To overhaul is to completely renovate, making major changes in order to repair. A few shutters and a new coat of paint won't come close to fixing that old, boarded-up house — it needs a complete overhaul.

overt.definition: open and observable; not secret or hidden
overt.short: Overt means open and done in plain sight. An overt demand for an increase in your allowance probably won't work; instead, try praising your parents' generosity and dropping hints about how high prices are these days.

overture.definition: orchestral music at the beginning of an opera or musical
overture.short: An overture is a piece of music played by an orchestra at the beginning of an opera or play. When an overture begins, the actors take their places and wait for the curtain to rise.

pan.short: A pan can be a cooking utensil, a negative opinion, or a sweeping camera shot. You cook eggs in a pan, but as a film critic you might pan a new docudrama for poor editing and overuse of pan shots.

parasite.definition: an animal or plant that lives in or on a host
parasite.short: A creature that lives off another organism is a parasite. The parasite might not hurt the host, but it doesn't do anything to help it, either.

parquetry.definition: a patterned wood inlay used to cover a floor

pathology.definition: the branch of medical science that studies diseases
pathology.short: Pathology is the medical term for the way a disease works. A tumor is removed by a doctor trained in surgery, but you need a doctor trained in pathology to tell you if the tumor is cancer.

pebble.short: Pebbles are the small, round stones you might find on a beach. If you want a pet rock, a smooth pebble would be a good choice.

peccadillo.short: A peccadillo is a minor offense or sin. Parents recognize that their kids have a few peccadilloes: they don't always remember to say please and thank you, don't put their dirty clothes in the hamper, and worst of all, they keep finding the

pedestrian.definition: a person who travels by foot
pedestrian.short: A pedestrian is someone traveling by foot. If you're a pedestrian, you will likely get annoyed at the drivers who don't stop so you can cross the street.

peer.short: A peer is someone at your own level. If you are a 10th grader, other high school students are your peers.

perch.definition: an elevated place serving as a seat
perch.short: A perch is a temporary resting place. In the middle of climbing a tree, you might find a good perch on a high branch. Bet some birds will be perching there too.

perfidy.short: If you shared your most embarrassing secrets with a friend who then told them to everyone he knows, his betrayal could be described as perfidy.

perpetuate.short: Some things should last forever and others should not be perpetuated at all. Things that should NOT be perpetuated? Ugly rumors, arms races, and your Aunt Martha's annual fruit cake.

perplex.definition: be a mystery or bewildering to
perplex.short: To perplex someone is to amaze, baffle, bewilder, dumbfound, flummox, mystify, or puzzle them. Perplexing things are hard to understand.

pestilence.definition: any epidemic disease with a high death rate
pestilence.short: Pestilence means a deadly and overwhelming disease that affects an entire community. The Black Plague, a disease that killed over thirty percent of Europe's population, was certainly a pestilence.

petition.definition: a formal request that something be submitted to an authority
petition.short: If you're angry about the new rule that you have to wear bright pink shoes to work every day, why not write up a petition, get all your co-workers to sign it, and submit it to your boss.

petulance.short: Petulance is whininess and irritability. Your least favorite thing about the kids you babysit might be their petulance.

philanthropist.short: A philanthropist is a person who gives money or gifts to charities, or helps needy people in other ways. Famous examples include Andrew Carnegie and Bill & Melinda Gates.

pine.short: If you pine for someone, you desperately want to see them, be with them, or perhaps smother them with kisses. If you're texting your ex-boyfriend over 50 times a day, there's a pretty good chance that you still pine for him.

piquant.short: Feeling a little saucy? Perhaps a bit provocative — but in a good way? Then it's safe to say your personality is a little piquant.

pitch.definition: the high or low quality of a sound
pitch.short: In baseball, the ball is pitched (thrown). Elsewhere, writers, salesmen, and other folks make pitches (proposals).

pith.definition: spongelike central cylinder of the stems of flowering plants
pith.short: Pith is the central idea or essence of something. If you’re in danger, you could exclaim, “I would greatly appreciate it if someone would provide assistance.” Or, you could get right to the pith of your point by shouting, “Help!”

placid.short: Call a body of water placid if it has a smooth surface and no waves. Call a person placid if they don't tend to make waves by causing a fuss.

plaintiff.definition: a person who brings an action in a court of law
plaintiff.short: In a courtroom, the plaintiff is the person or group who is accusing another person or group of some wrongdoing. If you’re the plaintiff, you are claiming that a law was broken, and you’re in court to present your case.

plaintive.short: Plaintive is an adjective for describing someone or something with a pleading, sorrowful, desperate tone. If you have ever heard the plaintive howl of a wolf, then you know what we are getting at here.

plea.definition: a humble request for help from someone in authority
plea.short: A plea is what you make when you're begging for something with a sense of urgency and emotion. While you wouldn't call asking for a hall pass a plea, you could make a plea for justice or world peace.

plod.definition: walk heavily and firmly, as when weary, or through mud
plod.short: When you plod, you walk slowly, heavily, and deliberately. You might need to plod through the snow to get to the bus stop on time.

pluck.short: To pluck is to pick or pull a single item out of many, like a flower or a hair. As a noun, pluck is energy or enthusiasm, even when things are looking grim.

polymath.definition: a person of great and varied learning
polymath.short: A polymath is a person who knows a lot about a lot of subjects. If your friend is not only a brilliant physics student but has also published a poetry collection and won prizes at political debates, you can describe her as a polymath.

ponderous.definition: having great mass and weight and unwieldiness
ponderous.short: When you call Frankenstein's monster ponderous, it's not because he likes to ponder the great questions of life. It's because he moves like a Mack truck, only slower and less gracefully.

posture.definition: the arrangement of the body and its limbs
posture.short: Posture is the way you position your body or arrange your limbs. So stand up straight, put your shoulders back, and lift that chin up.

precedence.definition: status established in order of importance or urgency
precedence.short: Notice the verb "precede" in the noun precedence, and you'll be able to remember that precedence is the act of preceding, of coming before — either in time or in rank.

precious.short: To call something precious is to describe it as highly valued. Think of a gemstone when you call your baby sister precious.

prerogative.definition: a right reserved exclusively by a person or group
prerogative.short: A prerogative is someone's special right or privilege. As Bobby Brown once sang, "I don't need permission / Make my own decisions / That's my prerogative."

prescience.definition: the power to foresee the future
prescience.short: Do you already know what happens tomorrow? Next week? Next year? If you can see into the future, then you have prescience.

presume.definition: take to be the case or to be true
presume.short: If you presume something, it means you act as though it's true before all the evidence is in. If you are presumed to be the fastest runner, don't get too cocky — that word implies that you haven't actually raced yet.

presumption.definition: a premise that is taken for granted
presumption.short: The noun presumption pretty much means jumping to conclusions. It is taking something for granted — an idea, an answer, an event — without having any real knowledge about it, and that is usually not a good thing.

proclamation.short: A proclamation is a formal public statement. If the mayor of your town issued a proclamation honoring the state champion field hockey team, this is an official statement of praise and celebration.

prodigality.short: Prodigality is excessive or extravagant spending. Your friend may feel he needs those gold chairs for his living room, but to everyone else it’s another example of his prodigality.

profligate.short: Profligate, as a noun or as an adjective, implies recklessly wasting your money on extravagant luxury. Profligate behavior is a lot of fun, but you'll regret it later — when you get your charge card bill.

profuse.definition: produced or growing in extreme abundance
profuse.short: Profuse is a word for a lot of something or even way too much — a profuse rainfall is a serious amount of rain.

prolong.definition: lengthen in time; cause to be or last longer
prolong.short: I don't want to prolong this definition, so I'll keep it short. To prolong is to make something last longer or to stretch it out in time.

prompt.definition: according to schedule or without delay
prompt.short: If you're prompt, you're on time. Go ahead and be prompt to class. Also, to prompt is to inspire or make happen. Getting in trouble for being late should prompt you to get a better alarm clock.

prone.short: If you're prone to doing something, it means you're likely to do it, have a habit of doing it, or are susceptible to it. People who are prone to getting the flu every winter should probably get a flu shot in the fall.

proposition.definition: a suggestion offered for acceptance or rejection
proposition.short: A proposition is a proposed plan of action, a detailed suggestion. You might consider your friend's suggestion to set up a neighborhood snow-shoveling business after the blizzard a winning proposition.

prosaic.short: Prosaic means ordinary or dull. Most of us lead a prosaic everyday life, sometimes interrupted by some drama or crisis.

protracted.short: Something protracted has been drawn out, usually in a tedious way. Protracted things are long and seem like they're never going to end.

provocation.definition: a means of arousing or stirring to action
provocation.short: A provocation makes someone angry or sometimes even violent. Telling a professional boxer how ugly he is would be considered a provocation — one that might get you punched in the face.

provocative.definition: serving or tending to excite or stimulate
provocative.short: If something is provocative, it provokes a reaction. A provocative book might get people talking about a controversial idea. A provocative statement, such as "I hate babies," will get another kind of reaction.

prune.definition: cultivate, tend, and cut back the growth of
prune.short: To prune means to clip, crop, cut back, and weed out. Pruning usually happens to overgrown trees and bushes, but can also be helpful for wild eyebrows and guest lists that are too long.

pugnacious.definition: ready and able to resort to force or violence
pugnacious.short: Pugnacious means ready for a fight. If you're pugnacious, you might find it hard to make friends. On the other hand, you might be a very successful professional boxer one day.

putrefy.short: When eggs rot, they putrefy or start to smell really, really bad. Putrefy is to begin stinking, usually when rotting or decomposing.

quack.definition: the harsh sound of a duck
quack.short: There are good quacks and bad quacks. A good quack is the sound a duck makes. A bad quack is someone pretending to be a doctor. (You'd be better off visiting the duck with your ailment.)

quarantine.definition: isolation to prevent the spread of infectious disease
quarantine.short: If you contract something highly infectious, such as pinkeye, please quarantine yourself so that you don’t infect others with it. This means you’ll have to stay in isolation and avoid contact with other people.

quash.short: Quash means to put down, stop, extinguish, and it’s usually used to talk about ideas, feelings, or political movements. You wouldn’t quash a grape underfoot; you would squash it. But if you were a military dictator, you would quash a revolu

quenching.definition: the act of extinguishing; causing to stop burning

quiescence.definition: a state of possibly temporary inaction or dormancy
quiescence.short: If there's a particular time of day when your ten kittens settle down to nap, you can call that their period of quiescence, or the time when they are all quiet and restful.

quintessential.definition: representing the perfect example of a class or quality
quintessential.short: If someone tells you you’re the quintessential rock musician, that means they think that everything about you screams “rock and roll.” Quintessential means embodying or possessing the essence of something.

quiver.short: Scaredy cats around the globe know that quivering is a trembling, shaking motion. A cold gust of wind might make you quiver, as would an icy glare from the abominable snowman.

ramification.definition: a consequence, especially one that causes complications
ramification.short: A ramification is an accidental consequence that complicates things. Remember that time you borrowed your father's car without asking? The ramification was that Dad missed an important meeting, his company went under, and he had to sell the

rampant.definition: occurring or increasing in an unrestrained way
rampant.short: Rampant means wild or out of control. Unruly children might run rampant at the supermarket, knocking cereal boxes off shelves and thoroughly annoying the customers.

ranger.definition: an official responsible for managing an area of forest
ranger.short: A ranger is a person who takes care of a park or a piece of land. A park ranger makes sure the rules are followed and provides information to visitors.

rarefy.definition: lessen the density or solidity of

rash.short: A rash is something that spreads like wild fire — red itchy skin or a series of unfortunate events. It can also describe an impulsive, wild decision.

rationale.definition: an explanation of the fundamental reasons
rationale.short: The rationale for something is the basic or underlying reason or explanation for it. This noun (pronounced "rash-uh-NAL") is usually used in the singular: What was the rationale behind his decision to quit?

recede.definition: pull back or move away or backward
recede.short: Recede means to pull back, retreat, or become faint or distant. Flood waters recede, as do glaciers, and even abstractions like "panic" and "hope." Think "receding hairline." (That means bald.)

reciprocal.definition: concerning each of two or more persons or things
reciprocal.short: Reciprocal describes something that's the same on both sides. If you and your sister are in a big fight on a long car trip, you might resolve it through a reciprocal agreement that you'll stop poking her and she'll stop reading road signs o

reckless.definition: marked by defiant disregard for danger or consequences
reckless.short: If you are reckless, you don't think or care about the consequences of your words or actions, like a reckless driver who speeds while texting, knitting, and eating a sandwich.

recompense.short: Say you loan a few bucks to a friend who is short on cash, and he wins the lottery. Presumably, he'll be able to recompense you, or pay you back what he owes.

redeem.definition: exchange or buy back for money; under threat
redeem.short: If you redeem yourself, you make up for wrongs by doing something that makes you seem good again, like when after being irritable and snappy with your grandmother, you redeem yourself by bringing her flowers and apologizing.

refurbish.definition: improve the appearance or functionality of
refurbish.short: Whether it’s a bike, an old computer, or a tennis stadium, when you refurbish something you clean it up and make it look or perform better.

reinstate.definition: bring back into original existence, function, or position
reinstate.short: When you reinstate someone, you return that person to a position or job. A principal might reinstate a laid off teacher when the school starts hiring again.

rejoicing.short: Rejoicing is celebration or festivity. There will be great rejoicing when your favorite cousin comes home after his three years away in the Peace Corps.

remorse.definition: a feeling of deep regret, usually for some misdeed
remorse.short: Remorse, a noun, is what you feel if you regret your actions or wish for another outcome.

reverberate.short: If you give a loud shout in a cavernous place, like a gym or a church, the sound of your voice will reverberate throughout the room. Reverberate means sound waves traveling back and forth, as in an echo.

rigor.short: When a school boasts of its academic rigor, it means its students learn a lot and work really hard. Rigor means thoroughness and exhaustiveness — the gold standard for a good teacher.

rotundity.short: When you talk about the rotundity, or roundness, of a voice, you're talking about how full-bodied and easily heard it is.  The rotundity of a person is how round they are in the belly. Any 3D object that has a roundness to it can be describ

salvage.definition: rescuing a ship or its crew from a shipwreck or a fire
salvage.short: To salvage something is to save it...before it's too late. You might try to salvage your damaged reputation by defending yourself, or salvage a burnt piece of toast by scraping off the black residue.

scattered.short: When things are scattered, they’re spread out all over the place. If the forecast calls for scattered showers, it’ll rain here and there.

shatter.short: When you shatter something, you smash it or break it into small bits. If your sister hits a baseball directly into the kitchen window, the window will shatter — and your sister will be in big trouble.

shirk.short: To shirk your responsibilities is to avoid dealing with them — like when you watch four consecutive hours of infomercials instead of facing your homework.

shrill.definition: having or emitting a high-pitched and sharp tone or tones
shrill.short: A shrill sound is high pitched and sharp, like the squeak of the mouse in your cupboard or the sound of your “eek” when you hear it.

shuck.definition: material consisting of seed coverings and small pieces of stem or leaves that have been separated from the seeds

shun.definition: avoid and stay away from deliberately
shun.short: If you purposely stay away from someone, you shun that person.  A sensitive baker may ask why you are shunning her cookies.

shunt.definition: a conductor diverting a fraction of current from a device
shunt.short: A shunt is a small tube that goes inside the body to drain fluid. It also means to divert in a general way, like if you shunt the thought of tubes in your body, you think about rainbows and kittens instead.

sinister.short: People who are left-handed might feel unlucky having to use a desk designed for right-handers, but there probably wasn't any sinister, or evil, intent behind the design. Or was there?

sip.short: When you drink a small amount of something, you sip it. If your cocoa is extremely hot, you should probably sip it until it cools off a little bit.

skeptical.definition: marked by or given to doubt
skeptical.short: If a friend told you that her family was perfect and they never had any problems, would you believe her? If not, you may be skeptical. Skeptical people look at the world with a certain amount of doubt.

sketchy.definition: giving only major points; lacking completeness
sketchy.short: Something sketchy is incomplete: it includes the major points but lacks detail. If a political candidate avoids going into detail about her political views, you might say her platform is sketchy.

skiff.definition: a small boat propelled by oars or by sails or by a motor
skiff.short: A skiff is a small boat. If you decide to purchase a skiff instead of giant yacht, you're probably someone who prefers the quiet, simple life. (Or maybe you just didn’t want to spend the money on a yacht.)

slack.short: If you slow down at the end of a race, you slack off. When you use slack this way, it means to reduce your speed, to be sluggish, or to be negligent.

sloppy.short: Sloppy means "untidy" or "messy." If your bedroom has clothes all over the floor, it's sloppy. And, if you end up with food all over your shirt every time you eat something, you're sloppy.

slur.short: A slur is an insulting remark. In a political campaign, it’s not unheard of for a candidate to launch a slur at her opponent, though doing so is usually frowned upon.

smother.definition: deprive of oxygen and prevent from breathing
smother.short: To smother is to overwhelm or suffocate. If you've ever had a boyfriend or girlfriend who calls you twenty five times an hour to check up on you, then you know what it means to be smothered by someone.

sober.definition: not affected by a chemical substance, especially alcohol
sober.short: This adjective means the opposite of playful or drunk. When attending a funeral, you are expected to be sober in both senses.

sordid.short: Describe a person's actions as sordid if they are so immoral or unethical that they seem dirty. Think of the worst parts of a bad soap opera!

spell.definition: write or name the letters that comprise the accepted form of
spell.short: A spell is a series of words that has magical powers. If you’re under a spell, then what you do is out of your control — your thoughts and actions are dictated by the spell.

spendthrift.definition: someone who spends money freely or wastefully
spendthrift.short: A spendthrift person is reckless and wasteful with his money. If you're a spendthrift, you might find yourself in debt. However, it might be fun to have a spendthrift friend who likes to treat you to expensive lunches and lavish gifts.

spine.definition: the series of vertebrae forming the backbone
spine.short: Your spine is your backbone. When you sit and stand with your spine straight, people will compliment you on your good posture.

stark.short: Stark means "complete or extreme," like the stark contrast between your music taste — punk and weird metal — and your mom's, with all her 1950's doo-wop favorites.

startle.short: To startle is to jump, like when you're surprised. If you’re sneaking through a dark room and step on a sleeping dog’s tail — you might startle  the dog. If she barks, that dog startles you right back!

steadfast.definition: marked by firm determination or resolution; not shakable
steadfast.short: Someone who is firm and determined in a belief or a position can be called steadfast in that view, like your mom when she thinks you really shouldn’t wear that outfit.

stickler.short: A stickler is someone who insists that things are done in a certain way. Say you’re getting married and want to write your own vows, but your partner’s mother demands that you have a traditional ceremony. The mother is a stickler for tradit

stiff.definition: incapable of or resistant to bending
stiff.short: Stiff things don’t bend or move easily. Dead bodies and tree branches are often stiff, and your back might feel stiff if you sit for too long. But to stiff the wait staff is to skip the tip. Bad idea.

stifled.short: Stifled is an adjective for anything that's been squashed or smothered. You might have a stifled ambition to be an astronaut that you never admitted to others.

stigmatize.definition: condemn or openly brand as disgraceful
stigmatize.short: If you stigmatize someone, you have given that person a label — and it's usually a label that is limiting in some way.

stockade.definition: fortification consisting of a fence set firmly for defense
stockade.short: A stockade is an enclosed pen used to herd cattle and other livestock. Stockades can also house people, in the sense of a penal camp. In both cases, the treatment tends to be on the rough side.

stoop.definition: bend one's back forward from the waist on down
stoop.short: Stoop means to lean your head and torso forward and down. If you're six feet tall and you tour a historical building, you'll have to stoop to get through the low doorways.

strain.short: The noun strain is a stretch, effort, or exertion. You can strain your ankle, or your little brother can strain your patience.

stray.definition: wander from a direct course or at random
stray.short: Little Red Riding Hood strayed from the path and ended up getting eaten by the Big Bad Wolf. When you stray, you wander off without paying attention to where you're going, and sometimes you get in trouble.

strive.short: To strive is to endeavor, reach, or strain for something above or beyond. We strive for self-improvement, a better world, or success in general.

subdue.definition: put down by force or intimidation
subdue.short: To subdue is to hold back, put down, or defeat. A Doberman can be subdued with a bone, but subduing a yapping toy poodle can be a mail carrier's greatest daily challenge.

suborn.definition: incite to commit a crime or an evil deed
suborn.short: One of the reasons Mafia bosses are so good at avoiding prison is that they know how to suborn witnesses and jurors — that is, to bribe people to lie. After all, it wouldn't be nice if an accident were to happen on the way to court, right?

subsequent.short: For something that comes after something else in time or order, choose the adjective subsequent. If the entire class fails an exam, the teacher will hopefully make subsequent ones a little easier.

succumb.short: Use the verb succumb to say that someone yields to something they've tried to fight off, such as despair, temptation, disease or injury.

supplicate.definition: ask for humbly or earnestly, as in prayer
supplicate.short: If you don’t get the grade you were hoping for on your paper, you could try to rewrite it, or you could meet with your teacher and supplicate. Your humble requests for an ‘A’ may or may not work.

susceptible.definition: yielding readily to or capable of
susceptible.short: If you are susceptible to something such as infections or earaches, it means you are likely to become sick with these things.

sway.short: Back and forth...back and forth...back and forth...are you sea-sick yet? The sway, or rocking motion, of a boat is too much for many stomachs.

swift.short: If you were the first one to reach the finish line in gym class, the coach might have called you swift. Swift means quick or fast-moving. A glance, a current, even a decision can be swift.

syncopated.short: In music, rhythms or beats that are unexpected or sound "off" in an interesting way are syncopated. Typically, a syncopated beat puts the stress where it wouldn't usually be.

taciturn.short: Someone who is taciturn is reserved, not loud and talkative. The word itself refers to the trait of reticence, of seeming aloof and uncommunicative. A taciturn person might be snobby, naturally quiet, or just shy.

tamp.short: To tamp is to push or pack down a loose or granular material. If you work as a barista, you'll learn to tamp espresso grounds before running hot water through them.

tangible.definition: perceptible by the senses, especially the sense of touch
tangible.short: When you can touch something, it's tangible: "I need tangible proof that aliens exist — I want to shake their little green hands!"

tapestry.definition: a wall hanging of heavy fabric with pictorial designs
tapestry.short: A tapestry is a picture woven into cloth. It's a decorative rug you hang on the wall, with detailed images or designs on it. Some tapestries, like the famous Unicorn Tapestries, tell stories with their pictures.

tarnish.definition: make or become dirty or spotty
tarnish.short: To tarnish is to become dull or discolored. Silver tends to tarnish easily, which is why your mother is always having you polish the family silver.

taut.short: Taut means tight rather than slack. The tightrope ought to be taut and not dangling down by the lion cage.

temperate.short: Temperate means mild, moderate. If you're a temperate person, you are calm, reasonable. If you live in a temperate climate, it's warm and sunny, but not too hot.

tentative.definition: hesitant or lacking confidence; unsettled in mind or opinion
tentative.short: Choose the adjective tentative to describe something you are unsure or hesitant about. On Monday, you can make tentative plans for the weekend, but it's too early to commit to one party or another.

tepid.short: Tepid means lukewarm or half-hearted. If the applause for your mime-on-a-unicycle performance was more tepid than enthusiastic, it might be time to find a new hobby.

topple.short: To topple something is to knock it down. A house of cards can be toppled — so can a government.

torment.definition: intense feelings of suffering; acute mental or physical pain
torment.short: By repeatedly trying to make someone miserable you torment them. The noun torment is the result of the verb torment.

torpid.definition: in a condition of biological rest or suspended animation
torpid.short: While the adjective torpid sounds a lot like the noun torpedo, it actually describes something slow or even inactive, like the torpedo that's just sitting around before it's launched.

traitor.definition: a person who says one thing and does another
traitor.short: A traitor says one thing but does another. If you promise a friend you'll keep his secret, but instead you blab it to everyone, you're a traitor.

trample.definition: tread or stomp heavily or roughly
trample.short: To trample is to forcefully walk right over something or someone. If you fall down during a footrace, another runner might trample you.

transgress.definition: act in disregard of laws, rules, contracts, or promises
transgress.short: When you go beyond the boundaries, either physically or morally, you transgress. A river will transgress its banks as it floods, and students who cheat transgress school rules.

tremor.definition: an involuntary vibration, as if from illness or fear
tremor.short: A tremor is a trembling or shaking in a person or the Earth. If you're scared about speaking in public you might have a tremor in your voice — or wish that an earthquake tremor would open up the floor and swallow you first.

trepidation.definition: a feeling of alarm or dread
trepidation.short: When plain old "fear" isn't enough to get across a deep feeling of dread about something on the horizon, use the more formal word trepidation.

truce.definition: a state of peace agreed to between opponents
truce.short: When two warring sides decide to call it quits, it's called a truce — an agreement to end the fighting.

unequivocal.definition: admitting of no doubt or misunderstanding
unequivocal.short: If there is no doubt about it, it's unequivocal. An unequivocal response to a marriage proposal? "Yes. Yes! A thousand times yes!"

untenable.definition: incapable of being defended or justified
untenable.short: If something is untenable, you can't defend it or justify it. If your disagreement with your teacher puts you in an untenable position, you better just admit you made a mistake and get on with it.

uphold.definition: stand up for; stick up for; of causes, principles, or ideals
uphold.short: When you uphold something, you stick up for or support it. Police officers are paid to uphold the law, and crazy Elvis fans uphold their belief that he's still alive out there somewhere.

uproar.definition: a state of commotion and noise and confusion
uproar.short: Any kind of noisy disturbance can be called an uproar. A large group of political protesters outside City Hall is likely to create an uproar.

vagrant.definition: a wanderer with no established residence or means of support
vagrant.short: A vagrant is someone who is homeless and poor and may wander from place to place. In fiction a vagrant often is a criminal, but a real-life vagrant might just be a person who has lost a job and family and lives off the streets with help fro

valiant.definition: having or showing heroism or courage
valiant.short: It was pretty courageous of the automaker Plymouth to name a car the "Valiant" in the 1960s and 70s because that term usually refers to a hero or describes a really determined, or valiant, effort that doesn't end well.

vanity.short: Vanity is the quality of being vain, or having a ridiculous amount of pride. If you have excessive vanity in your appearance, you probably spend long hours sitting at your vanity table doing your makeup or plucking your nose hairs.

varnish.definition: a coating that provides a hard, lustrous finish to a surface
varnish.short: Varnish is a shiny coating that's painted on a floor or piece of furniture and then dries clear and luminous. After refinishing an old oak chair, you might apply a coat of varnish. (Just don't sit on it until it's dry.)

vehemence.short: Something with vehemence is forceful and energetic. If you passionately believe that your neighbor is mistreating his dog, speak to him with vehemence.

veracity.short: Veracity sounds like some kind of disease you don't want to catch, but in fact, it means truthfulness. If you question the veracity of a statement or story, you wonder whether it is truthful or accurate.

vertigo.definition: a reeling sensation; a feeling that you are about to fall
vertigo.short: If you're standing still but the room is inexplicably spinning, you might want to let someone know you're suffering from vertigo — the sensation of dizziness or whirling.

vicious.short: Vicious is an adjective that means intentionally harmful or nasty. If you spread vicious rumors about a person, you're telling people things that will hurt her feelings or ruin her reputation. Cut it out!

vigilance.definition: the process of paying close and continuous attention
vigilance.short: Vigilance is devoted attentiveness or watchfulness. Security guards and test proctors alike need to practice vigilance.

vigor.short: If your new puppy runs all over the house, jumps on the furniture, and enthusiastically chews up your socks,  you may congratulate yourself on your new pup's vigor, or lively sense of energy.

vindicate.definition: show to be right by providing justification or proof
vindicate.short: Vindicate means to justify, prove, or reinforce an idea — or to absolve from guilt. If your family thinks you hogged the last piece of pie on Thanksgiving, you'll be vindicated when your younger brother fesses up.

vindictive.definition: disposed to seek revenge or intended for revenge
vindictive.short: It is no fun hanging out with vindictive people, who are forever out to get back at people they think have hurt them. If you forget to say hello to them one day in the hall, they will carry a grudge against you into next week.

vogue.definition: a current state of general acceptance and use
vogue.short: If something is the latest vogue, it is the latest fashion. When your new hairstyle catches on, it's in vogue —  or if it becomes unpopular, it’s not.

volubility.definition: the quality of being facile in speech and writing

voluptuous.definition: displaying luxury and furnishing gratification to the senses
voluptuous.short: The adjective voluptuous is used to describe someone who is attractive and curvy. Marilyn Monroe was known for her voluptuous figure.

voracious.definition: devouring or craving food in great quantities
voracious.short: Voracious is an adjective used to describe a wolflike appetite. It might be a craving for food or for something else, such as power, but the word usually denotes an unflattering greediness.

vulnerable.definition: capable of being wounded or hurt
vulnerable.short: Use the adjective vulnerable to describe something or someone open to being physically or emotionally wounded, like a newborn chick or an overly sensitive teenager.

wardrobe.definition: a piece of furniture that provides storage space for clothes
wardrobe.short: All of the clothes in your closet make up your wardrobe. If you're tired of your wardrobe,  it just might be time to go shopping.

waver.definition: pause or hold back in uncertainty or unwillingness
waver.short: To waver is to move back and forth, like when you waver, one minute thinking you'll stay home, planning to go meet your friends the next, until you finally make your decision.

weary.short: Weary as an adjective means "very tired or worn out," like weary students who finished a long week of studying and taking tests.

wile.definition: the use of tricks to deceive someone
wile.short: The noun wile is good for describing a particular kind of deviousness, but its plural form is most commonly used.

withhold.definition: hold back; refuse to hand over or share
withhold.short: If you keep something back and don't share it, you withhold it. You can withhold things such as permission, emotion, or information. You might get into trouble if you withhold information from your parents or the police.

abash.short: Although abash sounds like a big party or what firefighters do to get through a locked door, abash is, in fact, a verb that means you have caused another person to feel awkward, bashful, embarrassed, or ashamed.

abdicate.definition: give up power, duties, or obligations
abdicate.short: Sometimes someone in power might decide to give up that power and step down from his or her position. When they do that, they abdicate their authority, giving up all duties and perks of the job.

aberration.definition: a state or condition markedly different from the norm
aberration.short: An aberration is something strange that rarely occurs. An example of an aberration is when the temperature in Minnesota hits 90 degrees in January — it's nice and warm, but it's really strange.

abhor.short: If you abhor something, it gives you a feeling of complete hatred. Chances are you abhor that kid who used to torture the frogs in biology class.

abject.short: If it reeks of humiliation or looks like the lowest of lows, then you can safely describe it as abject.

abnegate.short: The verb abnegate means to deny or renounce something. Scientists abnegate the existence of little green spacemen from Mars.

abortive.definition: failing to accomplish an intended result
abortive.short: Did you ever start something and not finish it? If so, that was an abortive project. Abortive things don't get finished.

abridge.short: So the editor wants to cut your epic 800-page history of the stapler to a 150-page summary instead. Don't cry — he just wants to abridge your masterpiece, trimming it down to the more readable essential elements.

absolute.short: Use absolute as a noun or an adjective when you're so sure of something that you know it will never change. For example, a devout person's belief in life after death is an absolute; that person has absolute faith in the afterlife.

absolve.definition: grant remission of a sin to
absolve.short: To be absolved is to be let off the hook, to be set free from a certain obligation or to be forgiven for a wrongdoing. The Church may absolve you of your sins, but that won't absolve you of the need to attend mass.

abstinent.short: Someone who's abstinent is moderate and restrained, especially when it comes to consuming food and drink. A person who quits drinking alcohol completely is abstinent.

abstract.short: Use the adjective abstract for something that is not a material object or is general and not based on specific examples.

accost.definition: approach and speak to someone aggressively or insistently
